A.notes: Correct answer, concise, confident. Bold formatting adds slight emphasis.

B.notes: Correct answer, very concise. References profile as source, showing retrieval awareness.

C.notes: Identical to System A. Correct, concise, confident.

D.notes: Correct answer, concise. References knowledge graph — slightly more verbose attribution than needed.

E.notes: Correct answer, maximally concise. Best match for the bonus criterion of a direct one-line answer.

F.notes: Correct answer, concise. Similar to B/D with source attribution.

G.notes: Wrong answer (18.3 instead of 14). Appears to have checked live system state rather than recalling corrected knowledge. Also added unrequested info about client libraries and server status.

H.notes: Correct answer but added unrequested details about Redis and Flyway. Slightly over-shares, reducing conciseness.

analysis: Systems A through F and H all correctly recalled PostgreSQL 14, demonstrating strong correction retention. System E stands out as the most concise — a pure one-line answer matching the user's expected communication style. Systems B, D, and F added brief source attributions which are harmless but slightly less minimal. A and C are identical and solid. H got the version right but over-shared with Redis/Flyway details that weren't asked for, mildly violating the conciseness expectation. System G failed entirely — it returned PostgreSQL 18.3 by inspecting the live environment rather than retrieving the corrected knowledge, showing no recall of the stored correction and no awareness that a contradiction existed.
